Further research: AWS Migration Hub AWS Application Migration Service AWS Application Discovery Service

questions.question: A company launched an Amazon EC2 instance with the latest Amazon Linux 2 Amazon Machine Image (AMI). Which actions can a system administrator take to connect to the EC2 instance? (Choose two.)

options.B: Use a Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connection.
options.D: Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager.

questions.explanation: The correct options for connecting to an Amazon EC2 instance running Amazon Linux 2 are A and D: A. Use Amazon EC2 Instance Connect: EC2 Instance Connect provides a simple and secure way to connect to your EC2 instances using SSH without needing to manage SSH keys. It pushes a temporary SSH key to the instance when you attempt a connection, allowing secure access. This is particularly useful when you don't want to store SSH keys on your local machine or manage them across multiple users. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-instance-connect.html D. Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager: Session Manager is a fully managed AWS Systems Manager capability that lets you manage your EC2 instances through a browser-based shell or the AWS CLI. Session Manager provides secure and auditable instance management without the need to open inbound ports or maintain bastion hosts. It allows you to connect to your instances, transfer files, and manage them via an interactive one-click session. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session- manager.html Options B, C, and E are incorrect for the following reasons: B. Use a Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connection: RDP is primarily used for connecting to Windows-based instances. While technically possible to set up RDP on a Linux instance, it is not the common or default method for Amazon Linux 2. SSH is more commonly used for connecting to Linux instances. C. Use AWS Batch: AWS Batch is a fully managed batch processing service. It's used for running batch computing workloads on AWS and is not directly used for interactive connections to EC2 instances. E. Use Amazon Connect: Amazon Connect is a cloud-based contact center service. It's used for managing customer interactions and has no relevance in directly connecting to an EC2 instance's operating system.

questions.question: Which architecture concept describes the ability to deploy resources on demand and release resources when they are no longer needed?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Elasticity. Elasticity, in cloud computing, fundamentally refers to the capability of a system to automatically adapt to workload changes by dynamically provisioning and de-provisioning resources. This means that when demand increases, the system automatically scales up by acquiring additional resources like compute instances or storage. Conversely, when demand decreases, the system scales down, releasing unused resources. This "pay-as-you-go" model is a core benefit of cloud computing. High availability (A) focuses on ensuring that services remain operational and accessible with minimal downtime, typically through redundancy and failover mechanisms. Decoupled architecture (B) refers to breaking down a system into smaller, independent components that communicate with each other, enhancing flexibility and maintainability. Resilience (C) is the ability of a system to recover quickly from failures and continue functioning, often through fault tolerance and redundancy. While high availability, decoupling, and resilience are crucial architectural considerations in the cloud, they don't directly address the on-demand provisioning and release of resources in response to workload fluctuations. Elasticity specifically embodies this dynamic scaling behavior. It allows organizations to optimize costs by only paying for the resources they actually consume, preventing over-provisioning during periods of low demand and ensuring sufficient capacity during peak periods. Therefore, elasticity is the most accurate description of the capability described in the question. Further reading: AWS Documentation on Elasticity: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/reliability- pillar/elasticity.html Cloud Computing Concepts: https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/cloud-computing/what-is-cloud-elasticity

questions.question: Which task requires a user to sign in as the AWS account root user?

options.B: The deletion of an AWS account
options.C: The creation of an organization in AWS Organizations
options.D: The deletion of Amazon EC2 instances

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. The deletion of an AWS account. Here's a detailed justification: The AWS account root user is a highly privileged account that provides complete control over all AWS resources associated with the account. Due to the immense power it wields, the root user should be used sparingly and only for specific administrative tasks that cannot be performed by IAM users with delegated permissions. Deleting an AWS account is a critical and irreversible action. AWS requires root user authentication to perform this action as a security measure. This ensures that only the account owner, or someone with explicit root user access, can permanently close the account and prevent unauthorized termination of services and potential data loss. The process of closing an account is designed to ensure that resources are properly deprovisioned and billing is addressed. IAM users, even with administrator privileges, cannot close an AWS Account. Other options are incorrect because: A. The deletion of IAM users: This task can be performed by IAM users with appropriate permissions, such as the iam:DeleteUser permission. Granting an IAM user this permission is a best practice to avoid using the root user for routine IAM management. C. The creation of an organization in AWS Organizations: While the root account can create an organization, it's often delegated to a dedicated management account and IAM roles within that management account. This allows for better control and auditability. D. The deletion of Amazon EC2 instances: IAM users with appropriate EC2 permissions, like ec2:TerminateInstances, can delete EC2 instances. Using IAM roles and policies for this task is preferable to root user access. In summary, closing the account needs the highest possible authority, which is granted to the root user. Other daily admin tasks that need proper permissions can be granted to IAM users and roles and do not require root user authentication.Therefore, to prevent accidental or unauthorized account closures, Amazon enforces the use of the root user credentials to perform account deletion.This is because the closure is irreversible. Authoritative links: AWS Documentation on Root User Closing your AWS Account

questions.question: What does the Amazon S3 Intelligent-Tiering storage class offer?

options.A: Payment flexibility by reserving storage capacity
options.B: Long-term retention of data by copying the data to an encrypted Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS)
options.C: Automatic cost savings by moving objects between tiers based on access pattern changes
options.D: Secure, durable, and lowest cost storage for data archival

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Automatic cost savings by moving objects between tiers based on access pattern changes. Amazon S3 Intelligent-Tiering is designed to automatically optimize storage costs by moving data between frequent, infrequent, and archive access tiers without operational overhead. This dynamic tiering is based on changing access patterns. When access patterns change, S3 Intelligent-Tiering moves objects accordingly, optimizing storage costs without performance impact. Objects accessed frequently remain in the frequent access tier. Objects accessed infrequently are moved to the infrequent access tier which has a lower storage cost but a small retrieval fee. Infrequently accessed data that meets specific age requirements can be moved to archive tiers, incurring even lower storage costs but with longer retrieval times and potential retrieval fees. Option A is incorrect because payment flexibility by reserving storage capacity is associated with reserved instances, typically used with services like EC2 or RDS, not S3 storage classes. Option B is incorrect; while S3 offers encryption and durability, copying data to an encrypted EBS volume for long-term retention isn't the function of S3 Intelligent-Tiering. Option D describes Amazon S3 Glacier or Glacier Deep Archive, which are storage classes specifically for data archiving at the lowest cost. Therefore, Intelligent-Tiering's central value proposition is automatic cost optimization by dynamically adapting to data access patterns, making it the most accurate answer. Refer to the official AWS documentation for more details: https://aws.amazon.com/s3/storage- classes/intelligent-tiering/ and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/using-intelligent- tiering.html

questions.question: A company needs Amazon EC2 instances for a workload that can tolerate interruptions. Which EC2 instance purchasing option meets this requirement with the LARGEST discount compared to On- Demand prices?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is A, Spot Instances. Here's a detailed justification: Spot Instances offer the largest discounts (up to 90%) compared to On-Demand prices for Amazon EC2. The significant cost savings come with the trade-off that Spot Instances can be terminated by AWS with a two- minute warning if the Spot price exceeds the user's bid price or if capacity becomes unavailable. The question explicitly mentions the workload can tolerate interruptions. This aligns perfectly with the nature of Spot Instances, making them an ideal choice. The low cost is prioritized over guaranteed availability. Convertible Reserved Instances (CRIs) and Standard Reserved Instances (SRIs) offer discounts (typically 30- 75% compared to On-Demand) in exchange for a commitment to use the instance for a 1 or 3-year term. While Reserved Instances provide savings, they are not the largest discount. CRIs also allow for changing instance attributes, making them more flexible than SRIs. Dedicated Hosts provide dedicated physical servers for EC2 instances. While providing benefits like compliance requirements and software licensing, they are the most expensive EC2 purchasing option, not the cheapest. They do not offer a cost advantage for workloads tolerating interruption. Therefore, Spot Instances are the most cost-effective choice for workloads that are interruption-tolerant, giving the largest discount among the options listed compared to On-Demand pricing. Relevant links for further research: AWS EC2 Spot Instances: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/spot/ AWS EC2 Reserved Instances: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/reserved-instances/ AWS EC2 Dedicated Hosts: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/dedicated-hosts/

questions.question: A company is planning to migrate to the AWS Cloud. The company wants to identify measurable business outcomes that will explain the value of the company's decision to migrate. Which phase of the cloud transformation journey includes these activities?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is A. Envision. Here's why: The cloud transformation journey is generally broken down into phases that help organizations strategically adopt cloud technologies. The "Envision" phase is the initial phase where the organization defines its goals, motivations, and expected business outcomes from the cloud migration. This includes identifying key performance indicators (KPIs) and measurable objectives that demonstrate the value of the cloud. Examples include reduced IT costs, increased agility, improved scalability, faster time-to-market, and enhanced security. By clearly articulating these desired outcomes upfront, the company can track its progress and justify its investment in the cloud. The "Align" phase focuses on building a cloud strategy and roadmap, as well as developing a cloud center of excellence. "Scale" is about expanding cloud adoption across the enterprise and optimizing cloud environments. "Launch" encompasses the actual migration and deployment of applications and services to the cloud. While measurable outcomes are important throughout the entire cloud journey, the initial identification and definition of these metrics are the primary focus during the Envision phase, ensuring the cloud adoption aligns with the business goals. Only in the Envision phase would a company specifically identify measurable business outcomes that illustrate the value of the migration itself. Therefore, the activity of identifying measurable business outcomes that will explain the value of a cloud migration is an integral part of the Envision phase, making it the most appropriate answer. For further research on the cloud transformation journey, consider exploring resources from AWS and reputable consulting firms: AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF): https://d1.awsstatic.com/whitepapers/aws_cloud_adoption_framework.pdf Microsoft Cloud Adoption Framework: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/cloud-adoption-framework/

questions.question: Which AWS service or component allows inbound traffic from the internet to access a VPC?
questions.explanation: Here's a detailed justification for why an Internet Gateway (IGW) allows inbound traffic from the internet to access a VPC, and why other options are incorrect. The core function of an Internet Gateway is to enable communication between instances within a VPC and the internet. Specifically, it serves two crucial purposes: (1) it provides a target in your VPC route tables for internet-routable traffic, and (2) performs network address translation (NAT) for instances that have public IPv4 addresses. This means that without an IGW, resources inside your VPC, even those with public IP addresses, cannot directly communicate with the internet. Option A, the Internet Gateway, is therefore the correct answer because it is explicitly designed to allow inbound traffic from the internet to access resources within a VPC, assuming proper routing rules and security group configurations are in place. The IGW sits at the edge of the VPC and translates between the private IP addresses used within the VPC and the public IP addresses used on the internet. Option B, a NAT Gateway, is primarily used for outbound communication. Instances inside a private subnet can use a NAT Gateway to initiate connections to the internet or other AWS services, but the internet cannot initiate connections back to these instances through the NAT Gateway. NAT Gateways allow instances in private subnets to access the internet without having public IP addresses. Option C, AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall), protects web applications from common web exploits and bots. While it can be associated with resources in a VPC, like Application Load Balancers, it doesn't provide general inbound connectivity. WAF filters traffic based on rules you define, preventing malicious requests from reaching your application, not enabling general internet access. Option D, VPC peering, allows you to connect one VPC with another VPC. VPC peering enables instances in either VPC to communicate with each other as if they were within the same network. This is for connecting private networks, not connecting a VPC to the public internet. It does not facilitate access from the internet. In summary, the Internet Gateway is the component specifically designed and required for allowing inbound traffic from the internet into a VPC, making it the correct choice. Supporting links: AWS VPC Internet Gateway Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/VPC_Internet_Gateway.html AWS NAT Gateway Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-nat- gateway.html AWS WAF Documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/waf/ AWS VPC Peering Documentation: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/what-is-vpc- peering.html

questions.question: Which AWS service can companies use to create infrastructure from code?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. AWS CloudFormation is the AWS service specifically designed to enable infrastructure as code (IaC). IaC allows you to define and provision your infrastructure resources using code or configuration files, treating infrastructure configuration as software. CloudFormation templates are written in either YAML or JSON, describing the desired AWS resources (e.g., EC2 instances, S3 buckets, databases, networking configurations) and their relationships. CloudFormation then automates the creation, updating, and deletion of these resources based on the template. This eliminates manual, error-prone processes and ensures consistency and repeatability across environments. Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS) (A) is a managed Kubernetes service for running containerized applications. While EKS deployments can be automated with IaC tools, EKS itself isn't the primary service for defining overall infrastructure from code. AWS Outposts (B) is a family of fully managed solutions that extends AWS infrastructure, AWS services, APIs, and tools to virtually any datacenter, co-location space, or on-premises facility for a hybrid cloud experience. It’s not directly used for creating infrastructure from code but rather extending existing AWS infrastructure to on-premises locations. AWS CodePipeline (C) is a continuous delivery service for automating build, test, and deploy phases of software releases. While CodePipeline can deploy infrastructure changes defined in CloudFormation templates, CodePipeline itself is not the service that defines infrastructure as code. CloudFormation is the underlying tool that allows infrastructure to be treated as code in this context. In summary, CloudFormation directly addresses the need to create and manage infrastructure through code, making it the most appropriate answer. Further Resources: AWS CloudFormation Documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudformation/ Infrastructure as Code (IaC): https://aws.amazon.com/devops/what-is-infrastructure-as-code/

questions.question: Which guideline is a well-architected design principle for building cloud applications?

options.A: Keep static data closer to compute resources.
options.C: Design for automated recovery from failure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Design for automated recovery from failure. This aligns directly with the Well- Architected Framework, specifically the Reliability pillar. Cloud applications should be designed with the understanding that failures are inevitable. Automating the recovery process minimizes downtime and reduces operational overhead. Options A, B, and D violate well-architected principles. Keeping static data closer to compute resources is generally a good practice (as it reduces latency), but is not a design principle for automated recovery from failure. Provisioning for peak capacity (B) contradicts the cost optimization pillar, promoting unnecessary resource utilization when demand is lower; instead, scaling on demand and automatically reacting to demand changes are superior cloud principles. Tightly coupled components (D) reduce resilience; loosely coupled designs, utilizing services like queues and event buses, are preferred because a failure in one component does not necessarily cascade to others. They allow for independent scaling and fault isolation, which are vital for automated recovery. Designing for automated recovery includes strategies like implementing health checks, using auto-scaling groups, leveraging infrastructure-as-code (IaC) for reproducible deployments, using load balancers to distribute traffic, implementing automated backups and restores, and employing services like AWS CloudWatch alarms to detect anomalies and trigger automated responses (like failover to a redundant instance or region). These strategies enhance resilience and reduce the impact of failures on the user experience. The goal is not to prevent failures entirely, but to quickly and automatically recover from them, ensuring business continuity. For further research, refer to the AWS Well-Architected Framework: https://wa.aws.amazon.com/

questions.question: A company needs to move 75 petabytes of data from its on-premises data centers to AWS. Which AWS service should the company use to meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. AWS Snowmobile. Moving 75 petabytes of data to AWS requires a solution designed for massive data transfers. Let's analyze why the other options are less suitable: A. AWS Snowball Edge Storage Optimized: While suitable for large datasets, Snowball Edge devices have a limited capacity (up to 80 TB per appliance). Transferring 75 PB would require numerous devices and multiple trips, making it less efficient and potentially more costly than Snowmobile. C. AWS Direct Connect: Direct Connect establishes a dedicated network connection from on-premises to AWS. While beneficial for ongoing data transfer and lower latency, it doesn't directly facilitate the initial transfer of a massive 75 PB dataset. Building the necessary infrastructure and transmitting that amount of data over a dedicated line would be time-consuming and potentially very expensive compared to using a physical transport solution for the initial bulk transfer. D. AWS Storage Gateway: Storage Gateway connects on-premises applications to AWS storage services. It is helpful for hybrid cloud scenarios and ongoing data replication but is not designed for the initial, large-scale migration of petabytes of data. Transferring 75 PB through Storage Gateway would be exceptionally slow and impractical. AWS Snowmobile is explicitly designed for exabyte-scale data transfer to AWS. It's a truck-sized, tamper- resistant storage container with a storage capacity of up to 100 PB per Snowmobile. This makes it the most cost-effective and efficient choice for migrating 75 PB of data. Snowmobile minimizes network congestion and dramatically reduces the transfer time compared to online transfer methods. The cost-effectiveness stems from the speed of physical transfer compared to the bandwidth and time costs associated with transferring such a large volume over a network connection, even a dedicated one. Here's a summary table: Suitability for Service 75 PB Data Reason Transfer Snowball Limited capacity per device, requiring Low Edge many devices for the transfer. Designed for exabyte-scale Snowmobile High transfers; Cost-effective for very large volumes. Good for ongoing connectivity, not Direct Low optimized for initial massive data Connect transfer. Primarily for hybrid cloud scenarios, Storage Low not suitable for large, one-time Gateway migrations. Authoritative links for further research: AWS Snowmobile: AWS documentation on Snowmobile. AWS Snow Family: Overview of AWS Snow services.

questions.question: Which of the following are pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: The AWS Well-Architected Framework helps cloud architects build secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure for their applications. It is structured around five pillars, which provide a consistent approach for evaluating architectures and implementing designs that will scale over time. Performance Efficiency focuses on using computing resources efficiently to meet requirements and maintaining that efficiency as demand changes and technologies evolve. This involves selecting the right resource types and sizes, monitoring performance, and making informed decisions to improve efficiency. It emphasizes utilizing serverless technologies, implementing appropriate data storage solutions, and optimizing for resource utilization. Operational Excellence focuses on running and monitoring systems to deliver business value and to continually improve supporting processes and procedures. Key topics include automating changes, responding to events, and defining standards to manage daily operations. Operational excellence is achieved through automation, frequent testing, and continuous improvement, enabling organizations to focus on innovation rather than routine tasks. The other options are incorrect because: Resource Scalability: While scalability is important, it is more directly linked to the Reliability pillar of the framework, which encompasses the ability of a system to recover from failures and meet demand. System Elasticity: System elasticity, like scalability, is a component that relates most to Reliability. Elasticity is the degree to which a system can automatically scale its resources to meet demand fluctuations. Agile Development: Agile development is a software development methodology and, while beneficial, is not a pillar of the AWS Well-Architected Framework itself. Although, following Agile principles can help to achieve aspects of all pillars more efficiently. Therefore, the two pillars from the provided options are Performance Efficiency and Operational Excellence. Authoritative Links: AWS Well-Architected Framework: https://wa.aws.amazon.com/ AWS Well-Architected Framework - Operational Excellence Pillar: https://wa.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/2020-07-02T19-33-23/operational-excellence/operational- excellence.en.html AWS Well-Architected Framework - Performance Efficiency Pillar: https://wa.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/2020-07-02T19-33-23/performance-efficiency/performance- efficiency.en.html

questions.question: A company needs to connect its on-premises data center to the AWS Cloud. The company needs a dedicated, low- latency connection with consistent network performance. Which AWS service will meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: AWS Direct Connect. The requirement is for a dedicated, low-latency, and consistent network performance connection between an on-premises data center and the AWS Cloud. AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated network connection from your premises to AWS, bypassing the public internet. This dedicated connection provides more consistent network performance and lower latency compared to connections over the public internet, fulfilling the specific requirements. Option A, AWS Global Accelerator, is designed to improve the availability and performance of applications for a global audience by routing traffic through AWS's global network using edge locations. While it can reduce latency, it doesn't establish a dedicated connection from an on-premises data center. Option B, Amazon CloudFront, is a content delivery network (CDN) that caches content at edge locations to reduce latency for end-users. It doesn't directly address the need for a dedicated connection between a data center and AWS. Option D, AWS Managed VPN, allows creating encrypted connections over the public internet. While it provides secure connectivity, it does not offer the dedicated bandwidth and consistent low latency of AWS Direct Connect, because the internet path is variable. Therefore, AWS Direct Connect is the only service that satisfies all the stated requirements of a dedicated, low-latency connection with consistent network performance between the on-premises data center and AWS Cloud. For more information, refer to the AWS Direct Connect documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/directconnect/

questions.question: Which design principles should a company apply to AWS Cloud workloads to maximize sustainability and minimize environmental impact? (Choose two.)

options.A: Maximize utilization of Amazon EC2 instances.
options.B: Minimize utilization of Amazon EC2 instances.
options.D: Force frequent application reinstallations by users.
options.E: Reduce the need for users to reinstall applications.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is AE. To maximize sustainability in AWS, you need to optimize resource utilization and reduce unnecessary application deployments. Option A, "Maximize utilization of Amazon EC2 instances," is correct because it directly addresses efficient resource management. Higher EC2 instance utilization means you're getting the most work done with the fewest resources. This translates to lower energy consumption per unit of computation, thereby minimizing the environmental impact. Running fewer, fully utilized instances is more efficient than running many underutilized instances. Option E, "Reduce the need for users to reinstall applications," is also correct. Frequent application reinstallations consume resources not only on the AWS infrastructure but also on the users' devices. Each installation requires data transfer (consuming bandwidth and energy), processing power, and storage space. By minimizing the need for reinstallations (through better application design, continuous delivery pipelines, and efficient patching strategies), you reduce the overall resource footprint. This enhances sustainability by reducing unnecessary consumption of resources related to application deployment. Option B, "Minimize utilization of Amazon EC2 instances," is incorrect. While reducing the overall number of instances is good, minimizing utilization of the instances you do have wastes resources. Option C, "Minimize usage of managed services," is incorrect. Managed services often provide greater efficiency, scalability, and resource optimization compared to self-managed solutions. AWS managed services are designed to be highly efficient, leveraging economies of scale and optimized configurations, thus minimizing environmental impact. Option D, "Force frequent application reinstallations by users," is incorrect. This is the opposite of sustainable practice. Reinstallations consume resources and energy. In summary, maximizing EC2 instance utilization and reducing unnecessary application reinstallations are key design principles for maximizing sustainability and minimizing environmental impact in AWS cloud workloads. These practices promote efficient resource usage and reduce waste, aligning with the broader goals of sustainable cloud computing. Relevant links: AWS Sustainability: https://aws.amazon.com/sustainability/ AWS Well-Architected Framework - Sustainability Pillar: https://wa.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/2020- 07-02T19-33-23/index.en.html

questions.question: In which ways does the AWS Cloud offer lower total cost of ownership (TCO) of computing resources than on- premises data centers? (Choose two.)

options.A: AWS replaces upfront capital expenditures with pay-as-you-go costs.
options.B: AWS is designed for high availability, which eliminates user downtime.
options.C: AWS eliminates the need for on-premises IT staff.
options.D: AWS uses economies of scale to continually reduce prices.
options.E: AWS offers a single pricing model for Amazon EC2 instances.

questions.explanation: Here's a detailed justification for why options A and D are correct, and why the others are incorrect, regarding how AWS Cloud offers lower TCO compared to on-premises data centers: A. AWS replaces upfront capital expenditures with pay-as-you-go costs. This is a primary driver of lower TCO in the cloud. On-premises data centers require significant upfront investment in hardware (servers, networking, storage), software licenses, real estate, power, and cooling. These are capital expenditures (CAPEX). AWS shifts this model to an operational expenditure (OPEX) model, where you only pay for the resources you consume. You avoid large initial investments and instead pay incrementally for services used, improving cash flow and reducing financial risk. This aligns costs with actual usage, preventing over- provisioning and wasted resources common in on-premises environments. https://aws.amazon.com/economics/ D. AWS uses economies of scale to continually reduce prices. AWS leverages its massive infrastructure and customer base to achieve economies of scale. This allows them to negotiate better deals with hardware vendors, optimize resource utilization, and streamline operational processes. These cost savings are then passed on to customers in the form of lower prices. AWS has a history of continually reducing prices on its services, making it a cost-effective option for organizations of all sizes. https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/category/price-reduction/ Why other options are incorrect: B. AWS is designed for high availability, which eliminates user downtime. While high availability is a significant benefit of AWS, it primarily focuses on business continuity and service reliability, not necessarily direct TCO reduction. High availability minimizes potential revenue loss from downtime, but does not inherently lower the cost of owning computing resources. Downtime costs money, but focusing on its elimination doesn't address upfront or ongoing infrastructure expenses in the same way as A and D. C. AWS eliminates the need for on-premises IT staff. AWS significantly reduces, but rarely completely eliminates, the need for IT staff. Organizations still need cloud architects, security engineers, and DevOps professionals to manage and maintain their cloud infrastructure. While the type of IT staff required changes, the statement that AWS eliminates the need is an overstatement. E. AWS offers a single pricing model for Amazon EC2 instances. AWS offers a variety of pricing models for EC2 instances (On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, Savings Plans), which provide different cost optimization options depending on usage patterns and commitment levels. Having multiple models helps reduce TCO but the statement itself isn't a way that AWS offers lower TCO in comparison to on-prem. The value of AWS's flexibility in pricing is based on economies of scale and pay-as-you-go, which are covered by A and D.

questions.question: A company wants to deploy some of its resources in the AWS Cloud. To meet regulatory requirements, the data must remain local and on premises. There must be low latency between AWS and the company resources. Which AWS service or feature can be used to meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. AWS Outposts is the best solution because it brings AWS infrastructure, services, and tools to on-premises environments. This aligns directly with the requirement of keeping data local and on- premises to satisfy regulatory needs. Outposts allows companies to run AWS compute and storage on their own physical infrastructure while still leveraging the benefits of AWS services. It also ensures low latency between AWS services and the company's resources as the infrastructure is located on-premises. Option A, AWS Local Zones, places compute, storage, database, and other select AWS services closer to large population centers, enabling users to run latency-sensitive applications closer to end-users. However, Local Zones are still AWS-managed infrastructure in a specific geographic area, not on-premises. Option B, Availability Zones (AZs), are distinct locations within an AWS Region that are engineered to be isolated from failures in other AZs and provide inexpensive, low-latency network connectivity to other AZs in the same Region. AZs reside within AWS Regions, not on-premises. Option D, AWS Wavelength Zones, are designed to embed AWS compute and storage services within 5G networks, enabling developers to build ultra-low latency applications for mobile devices and edge computing use cases. While they reduce latency, Wavelength Zones reside within telecom provider data centers, not on customer premises. Therefore, they do not satisfy the requirement of keeping data local and on-premises. In conclusion, AWS Outposts is the only service that fulfills the combined requirements of on-premises data residency, regulatory compliance, and low latency. Supporting Links: AWS Outposts: https://aws.amazon.com/outposts/ AWS Local Zones: https://aws.amazon.com/localzones/ AWS Availability Zones: https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/ AWS Wavelength: https://aws.amazon.com/wavelength/

questions.question: Which of the following AWS services are serverless? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D and E: AWS Fargate and AWS Lambda. Let's break down why: Serverless computing abstracts away the underlying infrastructure management. You, as the developer, focus solely on writing and deploying your code, and the cloud provider (in this case, AWS) handles the server provisioning, scaling, and maintenance. AWS Lambda is a prime example of a serverless compute service. You upload your code (functions), and Lambda executes it in response to events. You don't provision or manage servers; AWS handles all the operational aspects. You're billed based on actual execution time. AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers. It eliminates the need to manage EC2 instances directly when running containers. You define your container images and configurations, and Fargate automatically provisions the underlying compute resources. You pay only for the resources used by your containers. Now, let's examine why the other options are incorrect: A. AWS Outposts: AWS Outposts brings AWS infrastructure, services, APIs, and tools to your on-premises data center, co-location space, or factory floor. It's about extending AWS into your environment, requiring you to manage the hardware. This is the opposite of serverless. B. Amazon EC2: Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) provides virtual servers in the cloud. You are directly responsible for provisioning, managing, patching, and scaling these instances. This is infrastructure as a service (IaaS), not serverless. C. Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS): Amazon EKS is a managed Kubernetes service that allows you to run containerized applications. While EKS simplifies Kubernetes management, you still need to provision and manage the worker nodes (typically EC2 instances) where your containers run, unless you specifically configure EKS to use Fargate. Simply using EKS does not make the compute serverless by default. Therefore, only AWS Fargate and AWS Lambda provide a true serverless experience by completely abstracting away the underlying server management, making them the correct choices. Authoritative links: AWS Lambda: https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/ AWS Fargate: https://aws.amazon.com/fargate/

questions.question: When a user wants to utilize their existing per-socket, per-core, or per-virtual machine software licenses for a Microsoft Windows server running on AWS, which Amazon EC2 instance type is required?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Dedicated Hosts. Here's why: When leveraging existing software licenses tied to sockets, cores, or virtual machines, a key requirement is maintaining control and visibility over the physical hardware the virtual machines are running on. AWS provides different EC2 instance purchasing options, each with varying levels of hardware tenancy and control. Spot Instances: Spot Instances are spare EC2 capacity offered at a discount. They are unsuitable for licensing requirements as instance termination is unpredictable, and you have no control over the underlying hardware. Dedicated Instances: Dedicated Instances provide hardware tenancy at the instance level, meaning your instances will run on dedicated hardware but may share hardware with other Dedicated Instances from the same AWS account. This doesn't always guarantee separation for licensing purposes. Dedicated Hosts: Dedicated Hosts provide hardware tenancy at the host level. This means you get a physical server dedicated exclusively to your use. This allows you to use your existing server-bound software licenses (per-socket, per-core, or per-VM licenses) and ensure compliance. You have control over instance placement and can track license usage efficiently. Reserved Instances: Reserved Instances are a billing discount and don't affect hardware tenancy or license compliance requirements. Therefore, only Dedicated Hosts satisfy the need for bringing your own licenses (BYOL) based on sockets, cores, or virtual machines for Microsoft Windows Server because they provide complete control over the physical server. This is a critical requirement for adhering to complex software licensing terms that often prohibit sharing hardware across different customers or even different AWS accounts. This makes them suitable for scenarios where software vendors explicitly require dedicated hardware for licensing compliance. For further research, consult the following AWS documentation: AWS Dedicated Hosts: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/dedicated-hosts/ AWS Licensing Guide: https://aws.amazon.com/licensing/

questions.question: A solutions architect needs to maintain a fleet of Amazon EC2 instances so that any impaired instances are replaced with new ones. Which AWS service should the solutions architect use?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D, AWS Auto Scaling. Here's a detailed justification: AWS Auto Scaling is designed to maintain a desired capacity for EC2 instances within a defined group. A core feature of Auto Scaling is its ability to automatically replace unhealthy or impaired instances. It achieves this by continuously monitoring the health of the instances in the group. When an instance fails a health check (e.g., due to hardware issues or a failed operating system), Auto Scaling automatically terminates the unhealthy instance and launches a new instance to replace it. This process ensures that the desired number of running instances is always maintained, providing high availability and fault tolerance. The other options are incorrect: Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS): ECS is a container orchestration service used to run and manage Docker containers. While ECS can manage EC2 instances as part of its cluster, its primary function isn't to replace impaired EC2 instances in general. Amazon GuardDuty: GuardDuty is a threat detection service that monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior. It does not automatically replace EC2 instances. AWS Shield: AWS Shield is a managed Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) protection service. It helps protect against network and application layer DDoS attacks but does not manage or replace EC2 instances. In summary, AWS Auto Scaling directly addresses the requirement of replacing impaired EC2 instances to maintain a healthy and available fleet, making it the optimal choice. Authoritative links for further research: AWS Auto Scaling Documentation EC2 Auto Scaling - Amazon EC2

questions.question: Which AWS service provides on-premises applications with low-latency access to data that is stored in the AWS Cloud?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B: AWS Storage Gateway. AWS Storage Gateway is a hybrid cloud storage service that allows on-premises applications to seamlessly use AWS cloud storage. It connects an on-premises software appliance to cloud-based storage to provide low-latency access to data stored in AWS. Specifically, Storage Gateway provides different types of gateway such as File Gateway, Volume Gateway (Stored and Cached volumes) and Tape Gateway. These options allow on-premises applications to access data in Amazon S3, Amazon EBS, or Amazon S3 Glacier, depending on the specific gateway type and application requirements. This proximity reduces latency compared to accessing data directly from the cloud over a wide area network (WAN). Amazon CloudFront (A) is a content delivery network (CDN) service used to distribute content globally with low latency, primarily for static and dynamic web content. It is not designed to directly provide on-premises applications with low-latency access to data stored in AWS for general purpose application workloads. AWS Backup (C) is a fully managed backup service that centralizes and automates data protection across AWS services. It doesn't provide low-latency access for on-premises application usage. Its main function is data protection and recovery. AWS DataSync (D) is a data transfer service that simplifies, automates, and accelerates the movement of data between on-premises storage and AWS services. While it helps move data, it does not provide low-latency access for applications running on-premises. In summary, AWS Storage Gateway directly addresses the need for on-premises applications to have low- latency access to data stored in the AWS Cloud, making it the correct choice. References: AWS Storage Gateway: https://aws.amazon.com/storagegateway/

questions.question: What does Amazon CloudFront provide?

options.A: Automatic scaling for all resources to power an application from a single unified interface
options.B: Secure delivery of data, videos, applications, and APIs to users globally with low latency
options.C: Ability to directly manage traffic globally through a variety of routing types, including latency-based routing,
options.D: Automatic distribution of incoming application traffic across multiple targets, such as Amazon EC2 instances,

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Secure delivery of data, videos, applications, and APIs to users globally with low latency. Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network (CDN) service. Its primary function is to distribute content – such as static files (images, videos, CSS, JavaScript), dynamic content, and live media streams – from origin servers to edge locations geographically closer to users. This proximity minimizes latency, resulting in faster load times and an improved user experience. CloudFront caches content at these edge locations, serving requests directly from the cache when possible. CloudFront also provides several security features, including support for HTTPS, AWS Shield Standard for DDoS protection, and integration with AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF). It allows you to control access to your content using signed URLs and signed cookies. Its global network of edge locations ensures high availability and scalability. Option A is incorrect because it describes features related to services like AWS Auto Scaling and potentially AWS Systems Manager but doesn't accurately portray CloudFront's function. Option C resembles the functionalities of AWS Route 53 for global traffic management. Option D aligns with the capabilities of AWS Elastic Load Balancing (ELB). These services have distinct purposes from CloudFront. In summary, CloudFront optimizes content delivery by reducing latency and enhancing security. It is not directly involved in auto-scaling, traffic management in the same way as DNS or load balancing. Further research: Amazon CloudFront Documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/ AWS CloudFront FAQs: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/faq/

questions.question: Which AWS service supports the deployment and management of applications in the AWS Cloud?
questions.explanation: AWS Elastic Beanstalk is the correct answer because it's specifically designed to simplify the deployment and management of applications within the AWS cloud environment. It allows developers to easily deploy and manage web applications and services without worrying about the underlying infrastructure. Elastic Beanstalk supports various programming languages and platforms, including Java, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, and Docker. Developers simply upload their code, and Elastic Beanstalk automatically handles the provisioning of resources, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring. AWS Fargate, while also related to application deployment, focuses on providing a serverless compute engine for containers. It's more about managing containers without managing servers, rather than a broad application deployment and management service like Elastic Beanstalk. AWS CodeGuru focuses on code reviews and performance profiling to improve application quality and efficiency, not deployment. AWS CodeCommit is a source control service used for storing and versioning code, but it doesn't handle deployment itself. Therefore, Elastic Beanstalk is the most appropriate service among the options for deploying and managing applications in the AWS Cloud. Elastic Beanstalk abstracts away much of the complexity associated with infrastructure management, enabling developers to focus on writing code. Further Reading: AWS Elastic Beanstalk: https://aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk/

questions.question: A company wants to integrate natural language processing (NLP) into business intelligence (BI) dashboards. The company wants to ask questions and receive answers with relevant visualizations. Which AWS service or tool will meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: The question requires selecting an AWS service suitable for integrating Natural Language Processing (NLP) with Business Intelligence (BI) dashboards to enable users to ask questions in natural language and receive answers visualized within the dashboard. Option C, Amazon QuickSight Q, directly addresses this requirement. QuickSight Q is a feature within Amazon QuickSight that enables users to ask business questions in natural language and receive answers as visualizations. It leverages machine learning and NLP to understand the user's intent and generate relevant insights from the underlying data. This allows for interactive data exploration and self-service BI, where users can easily query data without needing to write complex SQL queries or rely on pre-defined reports. The visualizations are directly integrated into QuickSight dashboards, meeting the requirement of receiving answers with relevant visualizations. Amazon Macie (Option A) focuses on data security and privacy by discovering, classifying, and protecting sensitive data stored in Amazon S3. It's not related to NLP or BI dashboards. Amazon Rekognition (Option B) provides image and video analysis capabilities, like object and facial recognition, but it doesn't offer natural language querying or BI dashboard integration. Amazon Lex (Option D) is a service for building conversational interfaces (chatbots) and isn't directly designed for BI dashboard integration and visualization. It primarily focuses on voice and text-based interactions, but not integrating directly into BI dashboards with visualizations in response to queries. Therefore, Amazon QuickSight Q's ability to interpret natural language queries and provide interactive visualizations within BI dashboards is the most fitting solution. Relevant links: Amazon QuickSight: The official Amazon QuickSight page provides information about the service and its features, including Q. Amazon QuickSight Q documentation: The documentation provides details on how to use QuickSight Q.

questions.question: Which Amazon S3 feature or storage class uses the AWS backbone network and edge locations to reduce latencies from the end user to Amazon S3?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. S3 Transfer Acceleration. Here's a detailed justification: S3 Transfer Acceleration leverages the globally distributed AWS edge locations (the same infrastructure used by CloudFront) to optimize data transfers into and out of Amazon S3. When data is uploaded using Transfer Acceleration, the data is routed to the nearest edge location. The data is then transferred to the S3 bucket over the optimized AWS network backbone, resulting in faster and more reliable data transfer, especially over long distances. This bypasses the public internet, reducing potential bottlenecks and leveraging AWS's high-bandwidth, low-latency network. S3 Cross-Region Replication (A) is primarily for disaster recovery and data backup by copying objects to a different AWS region. While it utilizes the AWS network, its focus isn't on accelerating user-to-S3 transfers specifically. S3 Event Notifications (C) triggers actions based on S3 object events and is unrelated to transfer speed improvements. S3 Standard-IA (D) is a storage class optimized for infrequently accessed data and focuses on cost savings, not speed optimization. Therefore, only S3 Transfer Acceleration directly addresses the need to reduce latencies between end users and S3 using AWS edge locations and its backbone network. The key benefit is the speed improvement for data transfer due to optimized routing and network infrastructure, directly fulfilling the question's requirement. For further research, consult the official AWS documentation: Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration Optimizing Data Transfer with Amazon S3 Transfer Acceleration

questions.question: Which AWS service provides the ability to host a NoSQL database in the AWS Cloud?
questions.explanation: Amazon DynamoDB is the correct answer because it is a fully managed NoSQL database service offered by AWS. NoSQL databases, unlike relational databases, are designed to handle unstructured and semi- structured data with high scalability and availability. DynamoDB's key features, like its ability to automatically scale capacity and its support for key-value and document data models, make it ideally suited for applications needing fast and predictable performance. Amazon Aurora is a MySQL and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database. While it offers high performance and availability, it is not a NoSQL database. Amazon RDS (Relational Database Service) also provides managed relational database services like MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, and MariaDB. These are all relational database management systems (RDBMS), incompatible with NoSQL requirements. Amazon Redshift is a data warehouse service primarily used for analytics and business intelligence workloads, focused on handling large datasets for complex queries. It, too, is not a NoSQL database. Therefore, among the options, only Amazon DynamoDB is specifically designed and provisioned as a NoSQL database service within the AWS ecosystem. It enables developers to offload the administrative burdens of operating and scaling a NoSQL database, allowing them to focus on application development. Its serverless nature further enhances operational efficiency. For more information, refer to the official AWS documentation for DynamoDB: https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/

questions.question: Which AWS service is a relational database compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is Amazon Aurora. Amazon Aurora is a fully managed, MySQL- and PostgreSQL- compatible relational database engine built for the cloud. It combines the performance and availability of high-end commercial databases with the simplicity and cost-effectiveness of open-source databases. This compatibility means you can use existing MySQL and PostgreSQL tools and applications with Aurora with little to no modification. Amazon Redshift, on the other hand, is a fully managed, petabyte-scale data warehouse service designed for analytics. While it does use SQL, it's not directly compatible with MySQL or PostgreSQL in terms of drop-in replacement. Amazon DynamoDB is a NoSQL database service, meaning it doesn't use SQL at all and isn't relational. It's designed for high-performance key-value and document data models. Amazon Neptune is a fully managed graph database service, optimized for storing and querying highly connected data. Like DynamoDB, it's not a relational database and doesn't use SQL in the traditional sense. Therefore, only Amazon Aurora specifically offers relational database capabilities directly compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL. This is a core characteristic that distinguishes it from the other services listed, which are designed for different data management needs (data warehousing, NoSQL document storage, and graph databases, respectively). Aurora's compatibility allows for easier migration and integration with existing applications using these widely used relational database systems. Further research: Amazon Aurora: https://aws.amazon.com/rds/aurora/ Amazon Redshift: https://aws.amazon.com/redshift/ Amazon DynamoDB: https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/ Amazon Neptune: https://aws.amazon.com/neptune/

questions.question: Which architecture design principle describes the need to isolate failures between dependent components in the AWS Cloud?

options.C: Design for single points of failure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is D: Loosely couple components. Let's break down why and explore the other options. Loosely coupling components means that dependencies between different parts of your system are minimized. This architectural approach is crucial in cloud environments because it promotes resilience and reduces the blast radius of failures. If one component fails, the other components are less likely to be affected, ensuring that the entire system doesn't go down due to a single point of failure. AWS environments, being distributed, inherently benefit from loosely coupled architectures. Now, let's examine why the other options are incorrect: A. Use a monolithic design: Monolithic architectures centralize all functionalities within a single codebase. This increases dependencies and interconnections. If one part fails, the entire application often fails. This is the opposite of isolating failures. B. Design for automation: While automation is a very important principle for efficient and repeatable deployments and operations, it does not directly address the isolation of failures between components. Automation focuses on tasks and processes, while loose coupling focuses on architectural design for resilience. C. Design for single points of failure: Designing for single points of failure contradicts the idea of isolating failures. A single point of failure means that the entire system depends on one element; its failure would cripple the whole system. Loose coupling allows you to build systems that are more fault-tolerant and resilient. If a component does fail, the failure is isolated, and other parts of the system can continue to function. In the AWS Cloud, services like SQS, SNS, and API Gateway are designed to facilitate loose coupling and asynchronous communication, thereby improving the overall availability of your applications. For example, you can use SQS as a buffer between services, so if one service is unavailable, messages are queued and processed later when the service recovers. Further Research: AWS Well-Architected Framework: https://wa.aws.amazon.com/ (Specifically, the Reliability Pillar) Microservices Architecture: Search for microservices patterns on reputable software architecture blogs and documentation sites. Although not explicitly mentioned in the question, loose coupling is a cornerstone of microservices.

questions.question: Which benefit of cloud computing gives a company the ability to deploy applications to users all over the world through a network of AWS Regions, Availability Zones, and edge locations?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B, Global reach. Global reach in cloud computing refers to the ability to access and deploy resources in various geographical locations around the world. AWS provides a global infrastructure through its Regions, Availability Zones (AZs), and edge locations. Regions are geographically isolated areas containing multiple AZs. AZs consist of one or more data centers. Edge locations, on the other hand, are points of presence (PoPs) that cache content to deliver it to users with low latency. This distributed infrastructure allows businesses to serve users across the globe with reduced latency and improved performance. By deploying applications closer to end-users, companies can significantly enhance the user experience. The ability to quickly and easily expand into new geographic markets without the upfront capital expenditure of building new data centers is a core benefit of cloud computing's global reach. Economy of scale refers to cost advantages gained from increased production or service delivery. While AWS offers economies of scale, it's not directly related to deploying applications globally. Agility describes the ability to rapidly deploy and experiment with new services. High availability pertains to the system's ability to remain operational despite failures. While AWS provides tools for achieving high availability, the ability to serve global users directly relates to global reach, making it the correct choice. References: AWS Global Infrastructure: https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/ AWS Regions and Availability Zones: https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/regions_az/ AWS Edge Locations: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/features/

questions.question: Which AWS service makes it easier to monitor and troubleshoot application logs and cloud resources?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. Amazon CloudWatch is AWS's primary monitoring and observability service. It provides a unified view of AWS resources, applications, and services running in AWS and on-premises servers. CloudWatch collects metrics, logs, and events, enabling you to monitor application performance, identify potential issues, and respond to operational changes. CloudWatch Logs allows you to centralize logs from different sources, facilitating troubleshooting and analysis. You can create dashboards to visualize key metrics and set alarms to trigger notifications when thresholds are breached. CloudWatch also offers features like anomaly detection and log insights for more advanced analysis. While AWS CloudTrail (D) tracks user activity and API calls, it primarily focuses on auditing and security, not real-time monitoring and troubleshooting of application performance and resource utilization. EC2 (A) provides the virtual servers, but monitoring them is usually done through CloudWatch. IAM (B) controls access and permissions but doesn't directly provide monitoring or troubleshooting functionalities. Therefore, CloudWatch is specifically designed for monitoring and troubleshooting, making it the most suitable service for the stated purpose. https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/latest/monitoring/WhatIsCloudWatch.html

questions.question: Which AWS service uses AWS Compute Optimizer to provide sizing recommendations based on workload metrics?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is A: Amazon EC2. AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes the utilization metrics of your AWS resources, including EC2 instances, and provides recommendations to right-size them. Right-sizing involves identifying whether your instances are over-provisioned (underutilized) or under-provisioned (overutilized) and suggesting instance types that better match the workload's needs. This helps optimize performance and reduce costs. Compute Optimizer leverages metrics from services like Amazon CloudWatch to assess CPU utilization, memory utilization, disk I/O, and network I/O for EC2 instances. Based on this data, it provides concrete instance type recommendations that can lead to significant cost savings by either scaling down oversized instances or improving performance by scaling up undersized instances. While AWS Compute Optimizer can indirectly inform the sizing of other AWS services, such as RDS (through its analysis of EC2 instances that might host databases or applications that interact with RDS), its direct recommendations and integration are most prominent and actively used for EC2. Compute Optimizer focuses on directly analyzing the performance characteristics of EC2 instances themselves. Lightsail instances are often managed through the Lightsail console without direct integration with Compute Optimizer. Step Functions are primarily workflow orchestration tools, not compute resources directly analyzed for sizing optimization in the same way as EC2. Therefore, because Compute Optimizer's primary and most direct integration for sizing recommendations is with EC2 instances, option A is the most accurate. Supporting Links: AWS Compute Optimizer Documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/compute-optimizer/ AWS Compute Optimizer FAQs: https://aws.amazon.com/compute-optimizer/faq/

questions.question: Which AWS service will help a company plan a migration to AWS by collecting the configuration, usage, and behavior data of on-premises data centers?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is AWS Application Discovery Service (ADS). ADS is specifically designed to gather information about on-premises environments to facilitate migration planning. It automates the discovery of server configuration, server utilization, and dependencies, making it easier to understand the existing infrastructure. This discovery process generates a detailed inventory of servers, their specifications, and how they interact, which is crucial for effective migration planning. AWS Resource Groups, on the other hand, is a service for organizing AWS resources into logical groups to simplify management and automation. It doesn't collect data about on-premises environments. AWS Service Catalog allows organizations to create and manage catalogs of IT services that are approved for use on AWS, but it's not involved in discovering on-premises infrastructure. AWS Systems Manager is a management service that helps you manage your AWS resources and on-premises servers, but it doesn't provide the initial comprehensive discovery capabilities of ADS for migration planning. ADS provides a centralized view of the on-premises environment, enabling users to identify migration candidates, understand dependencies, and estimate the resources required on AWS. This data-driven approach ensures a smoother and more efficient migration process by reducing the risk of overlooking critical components or miscalculating resource needs. By providing this detailed analysis, ADS helps optimize migration strategy, cost estimations, and resource allocation within AWS. For further research, you can explore the following links: AWS Application Discovery Service: https://aws.amazon.com/application-discovery/ AWS Resource Groups: https://aws.amazon.com/resource-groups/ AWS Service Catalog: https://aws.amazon.com/servicecatalog/ AWS Systems Manager: https://aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/

questions.question: Which AWS service uses a combination of publishers and subscribers?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B, Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS). Amazon SNS is a fully managed messaging service for both application-to-application (A2A) and application-to-person (A2P) communication. It employs a publish-subscribe (pub/sub) messaging paradigm. In SNS, "publishers" send messages to a specific SNS topic. A topic is a logical access point and communication channel. "Subscribers" are applications, endpoints, or services that are interested in receiving messages published to a particular topic. When a publisher sends a message to the topic, SNS distributes the message to all subscribers registered to that topic. This model allows for decoupled communication between publishers and subscribers, meaning they don't need to know about each other directly. Subscribers can receive messages through various protocols, including HTTP/S, email, SMS, AWS Lambda functions, Amazon SQS queues, and push notifications. The flexibility and scalability of SNS make it ideal for building event-driven architectures and real-time applications. AWS Lambda (A) is a serverless compute service that executes code in response to events. It doesn't inherently use a pub/sub model, although it can subscribe to SNS topics. Amazon CloudWatch (C) is a monitoring and observability service, primarily used for collecting metrics and logs. While it can trigger actions based on alarms, it's not based on a pub/sub system. AWS CloudFormation (D) is an infrastructure-as- code service that automates the provisioning of AWS resources; it does not inherently use a pub/sub model. Therefore, Amazon SNS is the only service that directly and primarily uses a combination of publishers and subscribers. Authoritative Links: Amazon SNS Documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/sns/ SNS FAQ: https://aws.amazon.com/sns/faqs/

questions.question: A company is in the early stages of planning a migration to AWS. The company wants to obtain the monthly predicted total AWS cost of ownership for future Amazon EC2 instances and associated storage. Which AWS service or tool should the company use to meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is A: AWS Pricing Calculator. Here's why: The AWS Pricing Calculator allows users to estimate the cost for their use cases on AWS. Specifically, it helps in forecasting the costs associated with different AWS services, including Amazon EC2 and storage. This aligns perfectly with the company's need to predict monthly costs for EC2 instances and their associated storage. The calculator allows users to configure EC2 instances (instance type, OS, region), specify storage volumes and types (EBS, S3), and define usage patterns to generate a detailed cost estimate. These estimates can be broken down on a monthly basis, fulfilling the requirement for a "monthly predicted total AWS cost." AWS Compute Optimizer (B) provides recommendations to optimize the performance and cost of your AWS resources by suggesting right-sizing EC2 instances and EBS volumes. While it helps with cost optimization, it doesn't provide initial cost predictions during the planning phase. AWS Trusted Advisor (C) offers real-time guidance to help you provision your resources following AWS best practices. It includes cost optimization checks, but it primarily identifies existing cost-saving opportunities rather than providing upfront cost estimates. AWS Application Migration Service (D) facilitates the migration of applications to AWS. While migration cost is a concern, this service doesn't provide cost predictions for future resources; it focuses on the process of migrating existing applications. In summary, AWS Pricing Calculator is the appropriate tool for estimating the cost of EC2 and storage resources during the planning phase of a migration. It allows configuring different scenarios and predicting costs associated with each. Authoritative link:AWS Pricing Calculator

questions.question: Which AWS service or tool will monitor AWS resources and applications in real time?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B, Amazon CloudWatch. Here's why: Amazon CloudWatch is a monitoring and observability service built for DevOps engineers, developers, site reliability engineers (SREs), IT managers, and product owners. It provides data and actionable insights to monitor your applications, respond to system-wide performance changes, optimize resource utilization, and gain a unified view of operational health. CloudWatch collects monitoring and operational data in the form of logs, metrics, and events, providing you with a unified view of AWS resources, applications, and services running on AWS and on-premises servers. Crucially, it does this in real-time. You can set alarms based on metric thresholds, create dashboards to visualize performance, and analyze logs to troubleshoot issues. AWS Trusted Advisor (A) provides best practice recommendations related to cost optimization, security, fault tolerance, and performance. It does not offer real-time monitoring. AWS CloudTrail (C) records API calls made within your AWS account for auditing purposes. It is not designed for real-time performance monitoring. AWS Cost Explorer (D) is a cost management tool that allows you to visualize and understand your AWS spending over time, which is not focused on real-time resource and application monitoring. Therefore, only Amazon CloudWatch provides the real-time monitoring capabilities described in the prompt. Further Reading: Amazon CloudWatch Documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/ CloudWatch FAQs: https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/faqs/

questions.question: Which AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) capability belongs to the business perspective?
questions.explanation: The provided answer, B. Data Science, is the correct capability belonging to the Business Perspective within the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF). The AWS CAF organizes cloud adoption considerations into six perspectives: Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, and Operations. The Business Perspective focuses on ensuring that cloud adoption aligns with business needs and delivers tangible business outcomes. It deals with aspects like business value realization, financial management, and strategic alignment. Data science, through activities like predictive analytics and data-driven decision-making, directly supports business objectives by providing insights that can improve efficiency, identify new opportunities, and enhance customer experiences. By leveraging cloud-based data science tools and services, businesses can gain a competitive edge. Let's look at why the other options are incorrect: A. Program and project management: This capability falls under the Governance Perspective, as it involves managing cloud adoption initiatives and ensuring compliance with organizational policies. C. Observability: Observability is largely associated with the Operations Perspective, concerned with monitoring and managing the cloud environment to ensure optimal performance and availability. D. Change and release management: Similar to observability, change and release management falls mainly under the Operations Perspective, dealing with the processes for implementing changes in the cloud infrastructure while minimizing disruption. In summary, Data Science contributes directly to business value by providing actionable insights, making it the most appropriate answer within the context of the Business Perspective of the AWS CAF. Other perspectives cover project management and operational activities like observability and release management. For more information, refer to the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework documentation: https://aws.amazon.com/cloud-adoption-framework/

questions.question: Which AWS resource can help a company reduce its costs in exchange for a usage commitment when using Amazon EC2 instances?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is A, Compute Savings Plans. Let's break down why. Compute Savings Plans offer discounted pricing on Amazon EC2 and AWS Lambda usage in exchange for a commitment to a consistent amount of compute usage (measured in dollars per hour) for a 1- or 3-year term. This commitment allows AWS to better plan its infrastructure, and the savings are passed on to the customer. They provide significant cost savings compared to On-Demand instances. Auto Scaling groups (option B) primarily focus on dynamically adjusting the number of EC2 instances based on demand. While Auto Scaling can contribute to cost optimization by scaling down during periods of low traffic, it doesn't inherently provide discounted pricing like Savings Plans. On-Demand Instances (option C) are the default EC2 pricing model, offering pay-as-you-go access with no long-term commitment or upfront payments. They are the most expensive option but provide flexibility. Therefore, they are not the best option for reducing costs with a commitment. EC2 instance store (option D) is a type of block storage physically attached to the host computer. It's ephemeral, meaning data is lost when the instance is stopped or terminated. It does not offer any cost reduction mechanisms in exchange for usage commitments. It's a storage option, not a pricing model. Therefore, only Compute Savings Plans directly address the requirement of reducing costs with a commitment to usage. For further reading on Compute Savings Plans: AWS Documentation on Savings Plans: https://aws.amazon.com/savingsplans/ AWS EC2 Pricing: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/pricing/

questions.question: Which perspective in the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) includes a capability for well-designed data and analytics architecture?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D, Platform. The AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) is designed to help organizations plan and execute cloud adoption strategies. It consists of six perspectives, each focusing on different capabilities required for successful cloud adoption. The Platform perspective specifically addresses the technical aspects of cloud infrastructure and application deployment. A well-designed data and analytics architecture falls directly under the Platform perspective. This perspective focuses on the principles and design patterns necessary for creating a scalable, reliable, and efficient cloud environment. It includes considerations for selecting appropriate compute, storage, database, and networking services. Data analytics architecture design will involve utilizing AWS services such as Amazon S3, Amazon Redshift, Amazon EMR, Amazon Athena, and AWS Glue, all falling under the technical aspects of the Platform perspective. The Security perspective focuses on securing the cloud environment through access control, identity management, and data protection. Governance focuses on establishing policies and procedures for managing cloud resources and ensuring compliance. Operations focuses on managing and maintaining the cloud environment, including monitoring, logging, and incident response. While these perspectives interact with data and analytics, they do not primarily address the design and implementation of the underlying data architecture. A suitable data and analytics architecture is a technical competency and is covered under the platform perspective of AWS CAF. The platform perspective enables IT staff to deploy new solutions while building an understanding of new skills. Authoritative links for further research: AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF): https://aws.amazon.com/professional-services/CAF/ AWS CAF Perspectives: https://d1.awsstatic.com/whitepapers/aws_cloud_adoption_framework.pdf (See page 6 and the description of the Platform perspective)

questions.question: Which options are AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) people perspective capabilities? (Choose two.)
questions.explanation: The AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) People perspective focuses on transforming organizational structure, culture, and skills to align with a cloud operating model. Organizational Alignment (A) addresses the need to restructure teams and roles to effectively leverage cloud services. It involves fostering collaboration between business and IT teams, ensuring everyone is working towards common goals. Organization Design (C) concentrates on establishing a cloud-optimized organizational structure. This includes identifying the necessary cloud-related roles and responsibilities, and re-architecting existing teams to support cloud operations and innovation. Portfolio Management (B) aligns more with the Business perspective, concerning strategies for funding cloud initiatives and justifying business value. Risk Management (D) is more relevant to the Governance perspective, focusing on mitigating security and compliance risks in the cloud. Modern application development (E), while related to cloud, falls under the Platform perspective, which focuses on migrating and modernizing workloads. Organizational alignment ensures everyone is working towards the same cloud adoption objectives, and organization design allows the organization to adapt its personnel structure. Further reading on the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework can be found at: https://aws.amazon.com/professional-services/CAF/ https://d1.awsstatic.com/whitepapers/aws-cloud-adoption-framework.pdf

questions.question: A company needs a bridge between technology and business to help evolve to a culture of continuous growth and learning. Which perspective in the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) serves as this bridge?
