options.C: Trade fixed expense for variable expense

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Increase speed and agility. Here's why: The scenario describes a company needing additional infrastructure quickly to support a new business venture. Cloud computing's inherent agility allows for rapid provisioning of resources, which perfectly addresses this need. Speed and agility in cloud computing refer to the ability to quickly deploy and scale resources on demand. Rather than procuring, installing, and configuring physical servers, which can take weeks or months, the company can leverage cloud services to spin up virtual servers and other necessary resources within minutes. This immediate access to infrastructure enables the company to rapidly develop and deploy its new line of business. Option A, "Benefit from massive economies of scale," refers to the cost advantages gained from cloud providers' large infrastructure, which is not the primary factor in addressing the time-sensitive need. Option C, "Trade fixed expense for variable expense," highlights the financial model of cloud computing, but doesn't directly address the speed of resource acquisition. Option D, "Go global in minutes," is more related to expanding geographically, not necessarily the initial provisioning of infrastructure for a new business line. Therefore, the rapid resource provisioning aspect of "Increase speed and agility" makes it the most pertinent advantage in this situation. The agility offered by cloud services allows companies to adapt quickly to changing business needs.https://aws.amazon.com/what-is-cloud-computing/https://azure.microsoft.com/en- us/overview/what-is-cloud-computing/

questions.question: A company that has an AWS Enterprise Support plan needs to protect its applications from DDoS attacks. The company requires access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT) 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.Which AWS service will meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: B. AWS Shield Advanced — Correct AWS Shield Advanced is the only AWS service that provides enhanced DDoS protection, includes 24/7 access to the AWS DDoS Response Team (DRT), and integrates with Enterprise Support. Shield Advanced offers advanced monitoring, attack diagnostics, real-time mitigation, and cost protection against large DDoS events. It is specifically designed for organizations that need continuous support and expert assistance during DDoS attacks. Because the company in the question needs both strong DDoS defense and direct access to the DRT, Shield Advanced is the only service that fulfills all these requirements. A. AWS Shield Standard — Incorrect AWS Shield Standard provides free, baseline DDoS protection for all AWS customers but does not include access to the AWS DDoS Response Team. It guards against common layer 3 and layer 4 attacks but lacks the advanced monitoring, detailed attack visibility, and personalized support that the company needs. Shield Standard alone cannot meet the requirement of 24/7 DRT access. C. AWS Firewall Manager — Incorrect Firewall Manager is a centralized security management service used to enforce AWS WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, and security policies across multiple AWS accounts. Firewall Manager does not itself provide DDoS protection or access to the DRT. It only becomes useful when combined with Shield Advanced —not as a standalone solution. Therefore, it does not meet the company’s requirements. D. AWS WAF — Incorrect AWS WAF protects applications from web-layer (L7) attacks such as SQL injection or cross-site scripting, but it does not provide DDoS protection by itself and does not include access to the DDoS Response Team. While WAF can help mitigate certain traffic-based threats, it does not meet the company's need for full DDoS protection and 24/7 expert support.

questions.question: A company wants to connect workloads that run on AWS to workloads that run on premises.Which deployment model will meet this requirement?
questions.explanation: A. Hybrid cloud — Correct A hybrid cloud deployment model integrates cloud-based resources (such as AWS services) with on-premises infrastructure. This model allows companies to run workloads across both environments and connect them through VPN, AWS Direct Connect, or other networking solutions. It provides flexibility, seamless communication, and workload portability between on-premises systems and the cloud. Because the company specifically needs to connect these two environments, the hybrid cloud model is the only option that fits the requirement. B. Private cloud — Incorrect A private cloud refers to cloud infrastructure that is run exclusively for a single organization, typically hosted on-premises or in a dedicated data center. While it offers high control and security, it does not involve connecting to a public cloud provider like AWS. Therefore, it does not meet the need to integrate on-premises workloads with AWS workloads. C. Cloud only — Incorrect A cloud-only deployment means running all workloads exclusively in the cloud, with no on-premises infrastructure involved. Since the company does have on-premises workloads and wants to connect them with AWS, a cloud-only model would not satisfy the requirement. D. Community cloud — Incorrect A community cloud is shared by several organizations with common concerns such as compliance or mission objectives. It is not designed for connecting a company’s AWS workloads with its own on-premises systems. This model does not address the integration the company needs.

questions.question: How do users benefit from the increased speed and agility of the AWS Cloud compared to traditional on-premises environments?

options.A: Users gain unlimited free storage for all applications.
options.B: Users can eliminate the need for technical IT staff.
options.C: Users can eliminate the time needed to plan workload capacity.
options.D: Users can reduce the time to provision IT resources.

questions.explanation: D. Users can reduce the time to provision IT resources — Correct One of the biggest advantages of AWS is the ability to provision resources in minutes instead of weeks or months. In a traditional on-premises environment, provisioning hardware requires purchasing, installing, configuring, and testing physical servers and networking equipment. With AWS, users can launch compute instances, databases, storage, and networking resources on demand with just a few clicks or API calls. This speed dramatically increases agility, enabling faster experimentation, quicker development cycles, and rapid scaling. Therefore, reduced provisioning time is the clearest benefit of AWS’s speed and agility. A. Users gain unlimited free storage for all applications — Incorrect AWS does not offer unlimited free storage. Although AWS provides scalable storage like Amazon S3, customers pay for the storage they use. This option does not relate to speed or agility and is factually incorrect. B. Users can eliminate the need for technical IT staff — Incorrect AWS reduces the need to manage physical infrastructure, but companies still need skilled IT staff to configure services, manage security, design architectures, and operate applications. Cloud computing shifts responsibilities—it does not eliminate them. Speed and agility do not remove the need for technical teams. C. Users can eliminate the time needed to plan workload capacity — Incorrect Although AWS reduces the burden of capacity planning by offering elasticity and auto scaling, it does not eliminate capacity planning entirely. Users still need to consider instance types, scaling policies, storage classes, throughput limits, and cost optimizations. The benefit of AWS is more about simplifying capacity planning, not eliminating it.

questions.question: Which responsibilities do the customer and AWS share according to the AWS shared responsibility model?
questions.explanation: C. Data encryption at rest — Correct Data encryption at rest is a shared responsibility between the customer and AWS. AWS is responsible for providing secure encryption mechanisms, managing the underlying infrastructure, and offering services such as AWS Key Management Service (KMS). Customers, however, must decide whether to enable encryption, manage their encryption keys (if using customer-managed keys), configure security policies, and ensure proper access control. Because both AWS and the customer play a role in securing and managing encrypted data, this responsibility falls under the shared responsibility model. A. Physical security for data centers — Incorrect Physical security of AWS data centers—such as controlling access, maintaining surveillance systems, and securing hardware—is 100% the responsibility of AWS. Customers have no access to AWS physical infrastructure and do not participate in these controls. B. AWS Region availability — Incorrect The availability and resilience of AWS Regions and Availability Zones is fully managed by AWS. This includes redundancy, power supply, cooling, network infrastructure, and the physical construction of data centers. Customers are responsible for architecting their applications for high availability, but Region availability itself is not shared—it is AWS’s responsibility. D. Networking hardware maintenance — Incorrect Maintenance of routers, switches, cabling, and all other physical networking hardware is entirely AWS’s responsibility. Customers manage logical network configurations such as VPC settings, security groups, and routing rules, but they do not maintain or repair any physical network components.

questions.question: A company needs to review its AWS Cloud expense history by department. Each department has its own AWS account.Which AWS feature or tool can the company use to meet this requirement?
questions.explanation: B. Cost Explorer — Correct AWS Cost Explorer is the best tool for reviewing historical AWS spending across multiple accounts, making it ideal for organizations where each department operates its own AWS account. When these accounts are linked under AWS Organizations with consolidated billing, Cost Explorer provides detailed cost breakdowns by account, service, usage type, tags, or custom cost allocation rules. This allows the company to track, analyze, and report departmental spending over time. Because the requirement involves reviewing expense history, Cost Explorer is the purpose-built tool for this type of multi-account financial visibility. A. AWS Budgets — Incorrect AWS Budgets helps set alerts and thresholds for cost or usage, but it is not designed for detailed historical analysis across departments. While useful for monitoring and notifications, it does not provide the level of cost history breakdown the question requires. C. AWS Purchase Order Management — Incorrect Purchase Order Management allows organizations to manage purchase orders for AWS billing, but it does not provide cost history analysis by department or account. It is purely administrative and not an expense analysis tool. D. AWS Cost Anomaly Detection — Incorrect Cost Anomaly Detection is used to identify unexpected spikes or unusual spending patterns, not to review historical costs by department. It is a monitoring and alerting service, not a reporting or analysis tool for departmental expenses.

questions.question: A company wants its workload to perform consistently and correctly. Which benefit of AWS Cloud computing does this goal represent?
questions.explanation: D. Reliability — Correct Reliability in AWS refers to the ability of a workload to perform its intended functions consistently, correctly, and without interruption. AWS provides a highly resilient global infrastructure with multiple Availability Zones, automatic failover capabilities, redundancy, and built-in fault tolerance. These features ensure that workloads continue running even when components fail. Because the company’s priority is consistent and correct performance, this directly aligns with the AWS benefit of reliability. AWS enables workloads to be architected in a way that maximizes uptime and minimizes the impact of failures, satisfying the company’s operational goal. A. Security — Incorrect Security refers to protecting data, applications, and infrastructure. While important, it does not address the goal of ensuring that workloads perform consistently and correctly. Security focuses on confidentiality, integrity, and access—not workload performance. B. Elasticity — Incorrect Elasticity allows workloads to automatically scale up or down based on demand. Although elasticity improves performance under fluctuating workloads, it is not the core benefit that ensures consistent and correct operation. That is specifically tied to reliability, not elasticity. C. Pay-as-you-go pricing — Incorrect Pay-as-you-go pricing is a cost model that allows customers to pay only for the resources they use. This has nothing to do with workload consistency, correctness, or operational performance. It is purely a financial benefit, not an operational one.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate to AWS. The company wants to avoid large upfront payments and minimize initial upfront costs during the IT infrastructure migration.Which benefit of the AWS Cloud will meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: C. Pay-as-you-go pricing — Correct The pay-as-you-go pricing model is one of the most significant financial benefits of the AWS Cloud. It allows companies to consume AWS resources on demand, paying only for actual usage rather than making large, upfront capital investments in hardware. This eliminates the need to purchase servers, storage devices, networking equipment, or data center space before migration. As a result, organizations can migrate with minimal initial cost and scale expenses gradually as workloads grow. This model is ideal for companies wanting to reduce risk, conserve cash flow, and avoid heavy up-front infrastructure spending. A. Global infrastructure — Incorrect AWS’s global infrastructure provides multiple Regions and Availability Zones for deploying applications worldwide. While valuable for performance and redundancy, it does not address upfront cost reduction or payment flexibility. B. High availability — Incorrect High availability refers to AWS’s ability to keep applications running reliably through redundancy and multi- AZ architecture. While important for uptime, it does not relate to minimizing upfront costs during migration. D. Managed services — Incorrect Managed services (such as RDS, DynamoDB, or Lambda) reduce operational overhead by offloading maintenance tasks to AWS. Although they may reduce ongoing management costs, they do not specifically address the goal of reducing upfront payments during the migration process.

questions.question: Which benefit of AWS Cloud computing provides lower latency between users and applications?
questions.explanation: C. Global reach — Correct AWS offers a massive global infrastructure with Regions and Availability Zones located around the world. By deploying applications closer to end users—geographically—AWS helps significantly reduce network latency. When workloads run in Regions near customers, data does not need to travel long distances, resulting in faster response times and an improved user experience. This is the essence of AWS’s global reach benefit: enabling low-latency access by hosting applications in the closest possible AWS Region to users. With hundreds of edge locations and local zones, AWS further enhances latency-sensitive workloads. A. Agility — Incorrect Agility refers to the ability to quickly build, test, and deploy applications. While agility accelerates development cycles, it does not reduce latency between users and applications. Latency improvements depend on infrastructure proximity, not speed of development. B. Economies of scale — Incorrect Economies of scale refer to cost savings achieved by AWS buying hardware in large quantities and passing savings to customers. This benefit affects pricing, not network latency or performance between users and applications. D. Pay-as-you-go pricing — Incorrect Pay-as-you-go pricing allows customers to pay only for resources they consume, reducing financial risk. It has nothing to do with latency or application proximity to users.

questions.question: A company is migrating their workload from an on-premises environment to AWS.Which costs will the company be able to eliminate with this migration? (Select TWO.)
questions.explanation: A. Data center costs — Correct Migrating to AWS removes the need for the company to maintain its own physical data center. This means no more expenses for powering servers, HVAC cooling, physical security, hardware procurement, racks, switches, cabling, or facility rental. All physical infrastructure is managed by AWS, so the company eliminates these major capital expenses entirely. This is one of the largest cost savings associated with cloud migration. C. Fixed costs — Correct Traditional on-premises environments require large upfront capital expenditures (CapEx) for purchasing hardware, storage systems, and networking equipment. These are fixed costs that must be paid regardless of how much the resources are used. By migrating to AWS, the company shifts from a CapEx model to a pay-as- you-go, variable cost model (OpEx). This means the company only pays for the compute, storage, and services it consumes, eliminating fixed infrastructure costs. B. Data security costs — Incorrect Although AWS provides security of the cloud, customers are still responsible for security in the cloud. This includes configuring IAM, encryption, logging, network rules, compliance, and protecting sensitive data. Cloud migration reduces some security costs but does not eliminate them. Security remains a shared responsibility. D. Monitoring costs — Incorrect Monitoring does not disappear in AWS. Companies still need monitoring tools such as CloudWatch, CloudTrail, or third-party services. In fact, monitoring becomes more important in cloud environments. AWS provides tools, but customers still pay for them and must manage dashboards, alerts, and logs. E. Training development costs — Incorrect Migrating to AWS often increases the need for employee training so teams can learn cloud architecture, services, security, and operations. Training costs are not eliminated—they usually become an essential and ongoing part of a cloud adoption strategy.

questions.question: A company migrated its core application onto multiple workloads in the AWS Cloud. The company wants to improve the application's reliability.Which cloud design principle should the company implement to achieve this goal?
questions.explanation: B. Decouple the components — Correct Decoupling components is a core AWS cloud design principle that significantly improves reliability. When components are tightly coupled, a failure in one part of the application can cascade and cause other components to fail. By decoupling—with queues, event-driven architectures, load balancers, asynchronous messaging, or microservices—each component can operate independently. This ensures that if one part of the system encounters an issue, the rest of the application can continue functioning normally. Decoupling increases fault isolation, improves resilience, and allows individual services to scale independently, making the overall application far more reliable. A. Maximize utilization — Incorrect Maximizing utilization refers to trying to use infrastructure as efficiently as possible—typically an on- premises mindset. In the cloud, this concept can actually reduce reliability by leaving no buffer for traffic spikes or unexpected load. It is not a design principle aimed at improving application reliability. C. Rightsize the resources — Incorrect Rightsizing helps optimize cost by selecting appropriate instance types, storage classes, or resource sizes. Although helpful for efficiency and cost control, rightsizing does not directly improve reliability. It focuses on cost optimization, not system resiliency. D. Adopt a consumption model — Incorrect Adopting a consumption model refers to paying for resources only when used. This is a financial and operational benefit, not a reliability principle. It has no effect on whether the application performs reliably under failure conditions.

questions.question: Which resource is always available at no cost to users?
questions.explanation: B. AWS re:Post — Correct AWS re:Post is a free, community-driven question-and-answer platform provided by AWS. It allows users to ask questions, share knowledge, and learn from AWS experts, partners, and other customers at no cost. Anyone with an AWS account—or even without one—can access re:Post to troubleshoot issues, understand best practices, and explore AWS services. Because it is always free and does not require a paid support plan, AWS re:Post is the correct answer. A. AWS Developer Support — Incorrect AWS Developer Support is a paid support plan. Although it is one of the lower-cost AWS support tiers, it is not free and requires a monthly fee. Therefore, it is not always available at no cost. C. The AWS Support API — Incorrect The AWS Support API is only available to customers with Business or Enterprise Support plans, which are paid services. Users must pay for these plans to access the Support API, so it is not free. D. An AWS Technical Account Manager (TAM) — Incorrect A Technical Account Manager (TAM) is provided only with Enterprise Support plans, which are premium, paid offerings. TAMs offer personalized guidance and proactive support, but they are not free resources.Key Takeaway:The only AWS resource that is always available for free is AWS re:Post, making it an excellent first stop for learning and community support.

questions.question: A company needs to run its existing custom, nonproduction workloads in the AWS Cloud quickly and cost- effectively. The workloads can recover from interruptions easily. Which pricing model should the company use?
questions.explanation: The company wants to run nonproduction workloads in AWS as quickly and cost-effectively as possible. These workloads are described as being able to recover easily from interruptions, which is a key requirement when selecting an AWS pricing model. In this scenario, minimizing cost is more important than guaranteeing uninterrupted compute availability. Option A, Reserved Instances ❌, is incorrect because Reserved Instances require a long-term commitment of one or three years. While they reduce costs compared to On-Demand, they are not ideal for workloads that need to start quickly or that do not require continuous availability. Option B, On-Demand Instances ❌, is incorrect because they provide maximum flexibility but at the highest cost. Since the workloads are nonproduction and fault-tolerant, paying full On-Demand pricing is unnecessary and not cost-effective. Option C, Spot Instances ✅, is correct because Spot Instances allow customers to use unused AWS capacity at significantly reduced prices. Although Spot Instances can be interrupted by AWS with little notice, the workloads in this scenario can easily recover from interruptions, making Spot Instances the best and most economical choice. Option D, Dedicated Hosts ❌, is incorrect because Dedicated Hosts are designed for compliance, licensing, or regulatory requirements and are the most expensive option. They are unnecessary for nonproduction, interruption-tolerant workloads.

questions.question: Why is an AWS Well-Architected review a critical part of the cloud design process?

options.A: A Well-Architected review is mandatory before a workload can run on AWS.
options.B: A Well-Architected review helps identify design gaps and helps evaluate design decisions and related
options.C: A Well-Architected review is an audit mechanism that is a part of requirements for service level agreements.
options.D: A Well-Architected review eliminates the need for ongoing auditing and compliance tests.

questions.explanation: An AWS Well-Architected review is a structured evaluation process that helps organizations assess how well their cloud workloads align with AWS best practices. It is designed to uncover potential risks, architectural weaknesses, and improvement opportunities early in the cloud design and implementation process. By reviewing workloads against the AWS Well-Architected Framework pillars, teams can make informed design decisions and build more secure, reliable, and efficient systems. Option A, A Well-Architected review is mandatory before a workload can run on AWS ❌, is incorrect because AWS does not require workloads to undergo a Well-Architected review before deployment. The review is optional but strongly recommended as a best practice. Option B, A Well-Architected review helps identify design gaps and helps evaluate design decisions and related documents ✅, is correct because the review focuses on validating architectural choices, uncovering risks, and ensuring alignment with AWS best practices. It provides actionable insights that improve design quality and documentation. Option C, A Well-Architected review is an audit mechanism that is a part of requirements for service level agreements ❌, is incorrect because the review is not an audit and is not tied to SLAs. It is a guidance and improvement tool rather than a compliance requirement. Option D, A Well-Architected review eliminates the need for ongoing auditing and compliance tests ❌, is incorrect because the review does not replace continuous governance, monitoring, or compliance activities. Ongoing reviews and audits are still required as workloads evolve.

questions.question: Which workload use case is appropriate for AWS Lambda?

options.A: Tasks that run without interruption for several days
options.B: Variable workloads with runtimes of less than 10 minutes
options.C: Deep learning tasks that require a GPU instance
options.D: Tasks that require a high performance computing (HPC) instance

questions.explanation: AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service designed to run short-lived, event-driven code without the need to manage servers. It is ideal for workloads that execute quickly, scale automatically, and do not require specialized hardware. Understanding Lambda’s execution limits is essential when deciding whether a workload is appropriate. Option A, Tasks that run without interruption for several days ❌, is incorrect because AWS Lambda has a maximum execution time limit of 15 minutes per invocation. Long-running tasks that require continuous execution are not suitable for Lambda and should instead use services like Amazon EC2 or AWS Batch. Option B, Variable workloads with runtimes of less than 10 minutes ✅, is correct because Lambda is optimized for short-duration workloads that run for seconds or minutes. It automatically scales with demand and is cost-effective for variable workloads, making it an ideal choice for this use case. Option C, Deep learning tasks that require a GPU instance ❌, is incorrect because AWS Lambda does not support GPU acceleration. Machine learning workloads that require GPUs must use services like Amazon EC2, Amazon SageMaker, or AWS Batch. Option D, Tasks that require a high performance computing (HPC) instance ❌, is incorrect because HPC workloads typically need tightly coupled compute resources, high memory, or specialized networking, which are not supported by AWS Lambda.

questions.question: What information is found on an AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) credential report? (Select TWO.)

options.A: The date and time when an IAM user's password was last used to sign in to the AWS Management Console.
options.B: The type of multi-factor authentication (MFA) device assigned to an IAM user.
options.C: The User-Agent browser identifier for each IAM user currently logged in.
options.D: Whether multi-factor authentication (MFA) has been enabled for an IAM user.
options.E: The number of incorrect login attempts by each IAM user in the previous 30 days.

questions.explanation: An AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) credential report is a security and compliance tool that provides detailed, account-wide information about IAM users and their credentials. It is commonly used to audit password usage, credential age, and MFA status to ensure adherence to security best practices. The report is generated in CSV format and focuses specifically on credential-related metadata, not real-time activity or behavioral analytics. Option A, The date and time when an IAM user's password was last used to sign in to the AWS Management Console ✅, is correct because the credential report includes a field that shows when a user last authenticated using their password. This helps security teams identify inactive users or credentials that should be rotated or disabled. Option B, The type of multi-factor authentication (MFA) device assigned to an IAM user ❌, is incorrect because the credential report only indicates whether MFA is enabled or not. It does not specify the MFA device type, such as virtual MFA, hardware key, or U2F device. Option C, The User-Agent browser identifier for each IAM user currently logged in ❌, is incorrect because IAM credential reports do not track session-level or browser-specific details. Such information is instead available in AWS CloudTrail logs, not in credential reports. Option D, Whether multi-factor authentication (MFA) has been enabled for an IAM user ✅, is correct because the credential report explicitly includes a column indicating the MFA status of each IAM user. This allows administrators to quickly verify compliance with MFA enforcement policies. Option E, The number of incorrect login attempts by each IAM user in the previous 30 days ❌, is incorrect because IAM credential reports do not track failed authentication attempts or security events. This type of data is monitored through services like CloudTrail, CloudWatch, or AWS Security Hub.

questions.question: A company runs its workloads on premises. The company wants to compare the cost of running its workloads on premises with the cost of running the workloads on AWS. Which AWS service will meet this requirement?
questions.explanation: When a company is running workloads on premises and wants to compare those costs with the cost of running the same workloads on AWS, it needs a tool that can estimate AWS pricing before migration. The AWS service designed specifically for this purpose is the AWS Pricing Calculator, which allows organizations to model their expected AWS usage and compare it against their existing infrastructure costs. Option A, AWS Cost Explorer ❌, is incorrect because Cost Explorer is used to analyze and visualize existing AWS costs and usage. It cannot estimate costs for workloads that have not yet been deployed to AWS. Option B, AWS Budgets ❌, is incorrect because Budgets is used to set spending limits and alerts for AWS usage after resources are running. It does not help estimate or compare costs for on-premises workloads. Option C, AWS Pricing Calculator ✅, is correct because it allows users to input workload specifications such as compute, storage, and data transfer requirements to estimate the cost of running those workloads on AWS. These estimates can then be compared with current on-premises costs to support migration and financial planning decisions. Option D, AWS Trusted Advisor ❌, is incorrect because Trusted Advisor provides recommendations for optimizing existing AWS environments in areas like cost, security, and performance. It does not compare on- premises costs with AWS pricing.

questions.question: A company wants to build and deploy applications that will serve users from around the world. The applications must perform with single-digit millisecond latency. Which AWS service or resource will meet these requirements?
questions.explanation: Option A, AWS Direct Connect ❌, is not suitable for this requirement because it provides a private, dedicated connection between an on-premises data center and AWS. While Direct Connect improves bandwidth consistency and reduces latency for hybrid environments, it does not accelerate access for global internet users and does not provide worldwide traffic optimization. Option B, AWS Global Accelerator ✅, directly addresses the requirement for global, low-latency access. Global Accelerator uses Anycast static IP addresses and routes user traffic to the closest AWS edge location. From there, traffic travels over the AWS global private network to the nearest healthy application endpoint. This approach reduces internet congestion, shortens network paths, and consistently delivers single-digit millisecond latency for users worldwide, regardless of their geographic location. Option C, AWS Config ❌, is a configuration management and compliance service that records and evaluates changes to AWS resources. It has no role in application traffic routing, performance optimization, or latency reduction. Option D, Security groups ❌, act as virtual firewalls that control inbound and outbound traffic to AWS resources. While they are essential for securing applications, they do not improve network performance or reduce latency for global users.

questions.question: A company wants to migrate an on-premises database to the AWS Cloud. The company has Microsoft SQL Server licenses for the on-premises database. The company wants to use the same licenses in the AWS Cloud. Which licensing strategy will meet these requirements?

options.A: Use Dedicated Hosts to follow a bring your own license (BYOL) strategy.
options.B: Purchase SQL Server licenses from AWS.
options.C: Convert the on-premises licenses to custom licenses.

questions.explanation: When a company migrates an on-premises Microsoft SQL Server database to AWS and wants to continue using its existing licenses, it must follow Microsoft’s licensing rules. Microsoft allows customers to reuse their existing licenses in the cloud under a Bring Your Own License (BYOL) model, but this is only permitted on specific AWS infrastructure that provides dedicated physical resources. Option A, Use Dedicated Hosts to follow a bring your own license (BYOL) strategy ✅, is correct because AWS Dedicated Hosts provide visibility and control over the underlying physical server. This satisfies Microsoft’s licensing requirements and allows the company to reuse its existing SQL Server licenses in AWS, often resulting in significant cost savings. Option B, Purchase SQL Server licenses from AWS ❌, is incorrect because this uses the license-included model. While valid, it does not meet the requirement of using the company’s existing on-premises licenses. Option C, Convert the on-premises licenses to custom licenses ❌, is incorrect because there is no such licensing mechanism in AWS or Microsoft SQL Server. Licenses must either be brought under BYOL or purchased as license-included. Option D, Migrate the database without licenses ❌, is incorrect because SQL Server always requires valid licenses. Running the database without proper licensing would violate Microsoft licensing terms. Thank you Thank you for being so interested in the premium exam material.
